The best book on software design I've read. Ousterhout argues that the primary goal of good design is to reduce complexity, and lays out concrete principles to get there.
A classic argument for why picking dull, well-understood tools is often the most intellectually honest engineering decision.

Why choosing proven, well-understood tools is often the most interesting engineering decision you can make.

Every few months, a new tool arrives promising to fix the thing that's been bothering you about the old tool. Sometimes it does. More often, it trades one set of problems for another set you haven't met yet.

This isn't an argument against new technology. It's an argument for knowing what you're choosing and why.

## The hidden cost of novelty

New tools have a real cost that rarely shows up in the benchmark post: you don't know where they fail yet. Every technology has failure modes. Postgres has them. Linux has them. The reason those failure modes don't destroy you is that millions of people have hit them before you, written about them, and built tooling around them.

With a new tool, you're the person who discovers the failure mode. That's expensive.

## When boring is the right call

Boring technology is right when:

- **The problem is well-understood.** If you need a relational database, you need a relational database. Postgres is boring. It's also extremely good.
- **The team needs to be able to debug it.** You can't debug something nobody understands.
- **The failure modes matter.** In production, boring is predictable. Predictable is safe.

## When it isn't

Boring technology is wrong when the boring option genuinely can't do the job, or when the overhead of the boring option is disproportionate to the problem. Using a full relational database for a config file is boring in the wrong way.

The skill is knowing the difference. That mostly comes from having been burned by both sides.

## The meta-point

The most interesting engineering decisions aren't about tools. They're about tradeoffs. Any experienced engineer can evaluate a tool. The harder question is: what are you optimizing for, and does this tool help you get there?

Answer that clearly and the tool choice usually follows.
A reflection on what draws me to building things and why the craft of software matters beyond the code itself.

There's a specific moment in every project that I keep coming back to. It's not the moment a feature ships, or when the tests finally go green. It's the moment when a problem that seemed impossibly tangled suddenly has a shape. When you can hold it in your head, turn it over, and see where the seams are.

That's why I write software.

## The thing about constraints

Software is almost entirely about constraints. Memory, time, bandwidth, backwards compatibility, the API you didn't design, the database schema from six years ago that everyone's afraid to touch. Most of the interesting work is finding a path through those constraints that doesn't feel like you've painted yourself into a corner.

Good constraints are clarifying. They tell you what matters. When you can't do everything, you have to decide what's actually important — and that decision is where design lives.

## Building versus shipping

There's a useful tension between *building* and *shipping*. Building is about getting something right. Shipping is about getting something out. The best engineers I've worked with are in constant negotiation between the two, never letting either one win completely.

The engineers who only build rarely ship anything anyone uses. The engineers who only ship accumulate debt that eventually makes shipping impossible.

The craft is in the negotiation.

## On writing about it

I write about software for the same reason I write software: to understand something better. Explaining a concept forces precision. It surfaces the gaps in your own mental model. It's a form of thinking out loud.

If any of this is useful to you, that's a bonus.
